content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 7, 1977

George M. Sanger, M.D.
Obstetrics and Gynecology
1337 Orange Avenue
Coronado, California 92118

Dear Dr. Sanger:

This is in reply to your inquiry of March 30, 1977. You ask whether you are required to disqualify yourself with respect to a pending decision of the Coronado Unified School District, of which you are a member, regarding Dr. Melvin Bensley. Mrs. Bensley was a patient of yours in 1971 and all fees for services rendered to Mrs. Bensley were paid in 1971.

Government Code Section 87100 provides:

87100. Public officials; State and Local. No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

Financial interest is defined as:

87103. Financial Interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made..."

As shown in the italicized portion of Government Code Section 87103(c), in order to invoke the disqualification provisions of the Act regarding a source of income, the official must make the decision which effects the interest, which is a source of income, within

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 · ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

George M. Sanger, M.D.
April 7, 1977
page two

twelve months of the time the income was received or promised to
the public official. Clearly, the 1971 payment of the Bensleys
would not now disqualify you from participating in decisions
involving Dr. Bensley. If you have further questions, please do not
hesitate to call me at 916/322-6444.

This letter constitutes staff advice pursuant to Government Code
Section 87114(b).

Sincerely,

Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

p ,,<>' -

F P P C
h,er 4 11 31 AM 77

GEORGE M. SANGER, M.D.
OBSTETRICS AND GYNECOLOGY
1337 ORANGE AVENUE
CORONADO, CALIFORNIA 92118

March 30, 1977

Fair Political Practices Committee
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Sirs:

As a duly elected member of the School Board of the Coronado
Unified School District I will soon be called upon to vote on an
appeal action which is contemplated in the matter of one of our
Elementary School Teachers, Dr. Marvin Bensley, who was recently
reinstated to his job by a Hearing Board on Professional Competence
which he requested.

Since his wife was a surgical patient of mine from 04-16-71
to 06-03-71, and I received $600.00 for services rendered to her,
my last professional contact being 06-03-71, and last payment
received on 06-03-71, I am inquiring to see whether my professional
contact with the Bensley Family in anyway could be construed as a
conflict of interest with regard to my vote in matters pertaining
to Mr. Bensley's employment.

An early reply would be greatly appreciated. If any further
information is required please call me at 435-3144, Collect.

Thank you,

[signature]
GEORGE M. SANGER, M.D.

GMS/ctv

SANGER          MEDICAL          CORPORATION

sections.question: As a duly elected member of the School Board of the Coronado Unified School District, whether you are required to disqualify yourself with respect to a pending decision regarding Dr. Melvin Bensley, whose wife (Mrs. Bensley) was your patient in 1971 with all fees paid in 1971.
sections.conclusion: Clearly, the 1971 payment of the Bensleys would not now disqualify you from participating in decisions involving Dr. Bensley. In order to invoke the disqualification provisions of the Act regarding a source of income, the official must make the decision which effects the interest within twelve months of the time the income was received or promised to the public official.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a school board member have a disqualifying financial interest in voting on employment matters concerning a teacher, when the teacher's spouse was the board member's patient six years earlier and all fees were paid in 1971?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the 1971 medical fees paid by Mrs. Bensley do not create a disqualifying financial interest because Government Code Section 87103(c) requires the decision to be made within twelve months of receiving the income, and more than six years have passed since the fees were paid.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Complete advice letter with both incoming request and FPPC response. The document includes the full statutory text cited (Gov. Code §87100 and §87103(c)). Clear question-and-answer structure with detailed legal reasoning. Signed by Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief of Conflicts of Interest Division.

QUESTION: As a duly elected member of the School Board of the Coronado Unified School District, whether you are required to disqualify yourself with respect to a pending decision regarding Dr. Melvin Bensley, whose wife (Mrs. Bensley) was your patient in 1971 with all fees paid in 1971.

CONCLUSION: Clearly, the 1971 payment of the Bensleys would not now disqualify you from participating in decisions involving Dr. Bensley. In order to invoke the disqualification provisions of the Act regarding a source of income, the official must make the decision which effects the interest within twelve months of the time the income was received or promised to the public official.

embedding.summary: Dr. Sanger, a school board member, sought FPPC advice regarding whether his prior patient-physician relationship with a teacher's wife (from 1971) created a conflict of interest in voting on the teacher's employment matter. The FPPC concluded no disqualification was required because the statutory twelve-month window for financial interest had long expired.
